findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Missing allowed-tools in manifest
findings.description: The SKILL.md manifest does not specify an 'allowed-tools' field. While this is optional per the spec, the skill's instructions describe extensive use of Bash (running Python scripts, spawning subagents, executing shell commands) and Write operations (creating files, writing JSON). The absence of declared tool restrictions means there is no manifest constraint to violate, but it also provides no transparency about what tools the skill expects to use.
findings.evidence: YAML frontmatter contains only name, description, license, and compatibility fields. No allowed-tools field is present.
findings.remediation: Consider adding an 'allowed-tools' field to the manifest that lists the tools the skill actually uses (e.g., [Read, Write, Bash, Python, Grep, Glob]) for transparency and to enable tool restriction validation.

findings.title: Command injection risk via subagent spawning with user-provided content
findings.description: The skill instructions direct the agent to spawn subagents with task prompts that include user-provided content (eval prompts, user queries). The instructions say to execute tasks like 'Skill path: <path-to-skill>' and 'Task: <eval prompt>' where the eval prompt comes from user input or test cases. This creates a risk of command injection if a malicious user provides crafted input that includes shell metacharacters or injection payloads.
findings.evidence: The instructions state: 'For each test case, spawn two subagents in the same turn — one with the skill, one without.' and 'Execute this task: - Skill path: <path-to-skill> - Task: <eval prompt>'. The eval prompt is user-provided and could contain injection payloads.
findings.remediation: Add input sanitization and validation for any user-provided content that gets passed to subagent tasks or shell commands. Use parameterized task execution rather than string interpolation.

findings.title: Tool poisoning risk via skill creation instructions
findings.description: The skill instructs the agent to create and modify other skills, including writing SKILL.md files with YAML frontmatter and markdown instructions. If a user requests creation of a malicious skill (e.g., one that exfiltrates data or bypasses safety), the skill creator could be used to generate harmful skills. While the instructions include a 'Principle of Lack of Surprise' section warning against creating malware, the enforcement relies entirely on the agent's judgment with no technical safeguards.
findings.evidence: The 'Skill Writing Guide' section provides detailed instructions for creating SKILL.md files, including writing YAML frontmatter and markdown instructions. The 'Principle of Lack of Surprise' section says 'skills must not contain malware, exploit code, or any content that could compromise system security' but provides no technical enforcement mechanism.
findings.remediation: Add validation checks when creating skills to scan for known malicious patterns (data exfiltration, credential theft, command injection). Consider sandboxing the skill creation process.

findings.title: Data exfiltration risk via eval viewer and feedback collection
findings.description: The skill's eval viewer (generate_review.py) collects user feedback and saves it to feedback.json. The instructions direct the agent to read this feedback file and use it to improve the skill. While this is a legitimate workflow, the pattern of collecting user data (feedback, eval results) and processing it could be exploited if a malicious skill were created that collects sensitive user data under the guise of 'feedback' or 'eval results'.
findings.evidence: The instructions state: 'When the user tells you they're done, read feedback.json' and 'Feedback will be downloaded as a feedback.json file when the user clicks Submit All Reviews'. The generate_review.py script saves feedback to the workspace directory.
findings.remediation: Ensure feedback collection is transparent to the user and limited to what's necessary for skill improvement. Consider adding data minimization practices.

findings.title: Indirect prompt injection via external reference files
findings.description: The skill instructions reference external files (agents/grader.md, agents/comparator.md, agents/analyzer.md, references/schemas.md) that contain instructions for subagents. These files are bundled with the skill package, so they are internal files. However, the skill also references reading 'transcripts' from subagent executions, which could contain user-provided content. The instructions say to 'Read the transcript' and 'Follow the skill's instructions' which could propagate injected content from transcripts.
findings.evidence: The instructions reference: 'Read agents/grader.md', 'Read agents/comparator.md', 'Read agents/analyzer.md'. The grader instructions say 'Read the transcript file completely' and 'Note the eval prompt, execution steps, and final result'. If a transcript contains injected instructions, they could be followed.
findings.remediation: Add explicit warnings in the instructions to not follow instructions found in transcripts or user-provided content. Treat all subagent outputs as untrusted data, not instructions.

findings.title: Potential obfuscation in generate_review.py
findings.description: The generate_review.py script uses base64 encoding for binary files (images, PDFs, spreadsheets) when embedding them in the HTML viewer. While this is a legitimate technique for embedding binary data in HTML, the pattern of base64 encoding could be used to obfuscate malicious payloads. The script itself is benign, but the technique could be misused.
findings.evidence: The script contains multiple instances of base64 encoding: 'b64 = base64.b64encode(raw).decode("ascii")' for images, PDFs, and binary files. These are used for legitimate embedding in HTML output.
findings.remediation: No action needed for this specific script, but be aware that the base64 encoding pattern could be used maliciously in other contexts.

findings.title: Potential resource exhaustion via unbounded iteration
findings.description: The skill instructions describe an iterative improvement loop that continues 'until you're satisfied' or 'until the user says they're happy'. While the run_loop.py script has a max-iterations parameter (default 5), the SKILL.md instructions themselves describe an unbounded loop with no hard stop. This could lead to excessive resource consumption if the agent gets stuck in an improvement cycle.
findings.evidence: The instructions state: 'Repeat until you're satisfied' and 'Keep going until: The user says they're happy, The feedback is all empty, You're not making meaningful progress'. These are subjective stopping criteria with no hard limit.
findings.remediation: Add explicit iteration limits or timeouts to the skill instructions to prevent unbounded resource consumption.
